target.title: [codex] active-memory default to configured agents
target.body:
## Summary

- Default Active Memory to configured OpenClaw agents when `plugins.entries.active-memory.config.agents` is omitted.
- Fall back to the implicit `main` agent for minimal configs that do not define `agents.list`.
- Preserve explicit empty `config.agents: []` as no targeted agents.
- Preserve explicit non-empty `config.agents` allowlist behavior.
- Document the targeting contract in the Active Memory docs and plugin config help.

This matters because omitting `config.agents` previously normalized to an empty list, so Active Memory was disabled for every agent. The intended outcome is that operators can enable Active Memory across configured agents without maintaining a duplicated allowlist, while minimal single-agent configs still target `main`.

Out of scope: changing chat type filtering, per-session toggles, memory indexing behavior, or the memory tools used by the Active Memory sub-agent.

Review focus: whether the fallback to `agents.list[].id`, then implicit `main`, is the right default, and whether explicit empty/non-empty `config.agents` remains unchanged.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #82528

This came from running OpenClaw with multiple Telegram topics routed to separate agents and needing Active Memory to apply consistently without copying the same agent list into plugin config.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed:
  Omitting `plugins.entries.active-memory.config.agents` normalized to an empty allowlist, so Active Memory skipped every agent instead of applying to configured agents.
- Real environment tested:
  OpenClaw 2026.6.5 on a Linux host with a systemd user gateway, Telegram group topics routed to `main`, `sementes`, `cloudfarm`, and `pedro-agenda`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  Applied the equivalent runtime change locally, removed `active-memory.config.agents`, restarted `openclaw-gateway`, then checked gateway status and memory search.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
openclaw gateway status --deep
Gateway version: 2026.6.5
Runtime: running
Capability: admin-capable
```

```text
journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since "3 minutes ago"
[gateway] http server listening (12 plugins: active-memory, brave, browser, canvas, codex, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, openai, phone-control, talk-voice, telegram; 12.1s)
```

```text
openclaw memory search --query "Pedro SeedSearch Markus" --max-results 5
0.545 memory/pendencias-grupo.md:1-21
0.516 memory/2026-03-31.md:1-37
0.453 memory/dreaming/light/2026-06-05.md:43-72
```

- Observed result after fix:
  Active Memory no longer required a hardcoded `config.agents` list to apply across configured agents.
- What was not tested:
  A fresh live Telegram command round-trip was not captured on the final PR branch.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  The production server was restored to upstream behavior after proof collection, using an explicit `config.agents` list until this PR is merged upstream.
- Before evidence:
  Reading the prior plugin implementation showed `normalizePluginConfig({}).agents` became `[]`, while `isEnabledForAgent` requires `config.agents.includes(agentId)`.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
pnpm docs:list
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts --maxWorkers=1
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts docs/concepts/active-memory.md extensions/active-memory/openclaw.plugin.json
pnpm exec oxlint extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts
node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/concepts/active-memory.md
node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check
node --import tsx -e "import { testing } from './extensions/active-memory/index.ts'; const cfg={agents:{list:[{id:'main'},{id:'support'}]}}; console.log(JSON.stringify({ omitted: testing.normalizePluginConfig({}, cfg).agents, empty: testing.normalizePluginConfig({agents: []}, cfg).agents, explicit: testing.normalizePluginConfig({agents:['support']}, cfg).agents, implicitMain: testing.normalizePluginConfig({}).agents }, null, 2));"
git diff --check upstream/main -- docs/concepts/active-memory.md extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts extensions/active-memory/openclaw.plugin.json
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode commit --commit HEAD
```

Regression coverage added:

- `defaults to all configured agents when no active-memory agent allowlist is set`
- `preserves an empty active-memory agent allowlist`
- `defaults to the implicit main agent when no agent list is configured`
- `keeps an explicit active-memory agent allowlist`

CI after the latest push:

- Pending for commit `b7e75ed8eaa7`. Prior CI was green on `fdf408d1e55464aced878f0fee85201324590de3`.

Final-head normalization proof on `b7e75ed8eaa7`:

```json
{
  "omitted": [
    "main",
    "support"
  ],
  "empty": [],
  "explicit": [
    "support"
  ],
  "implicitMain": [
    "main"
  ]
}
```

Autoreview note:

- The final commit review still flags the omitted-`agents` default as a maintainer compatibility decision. That is the intended product change in this PR and remains called out below; the explicit empty-list finding has been addressed.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

Highest-risk area:
Operators who intentionally omitted `config.agents` expecting Active Memory to run nowhere would now get Active Memory for configured agents, or `main` in minimal configs. Explicit `config.agents: []` continues to target no agents.

Risk mitigation:
Explicit `config.agents: []` remains a no-target allowlist, and explicit non-empty `config.agents` still behaves as an allowlist. Operators can set `enabled: false`, disable the plugin entry, set `agents: []`, or provide a narrow `agents` list when they want less coverage. The docs and manifest help now state the omitted default and the explicit-empty behavior.

## Current review state

Next action:
Await maintainer compatibility decision for omitted `config.agents` and CI completion on the latest commit.

Still waiting on:
GitHub PR checks for the latest push and maintainer decision on the omitted-agent default policy.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed:
Filled in real behavior proof after the `triage: needs-real-behavior-proof` label was applied, then added docs/manifest alignment and focused regression coverage for omitted, explicit-empty, implicit-main, and explicit allowlist targeting. The latest update preserves explicit `agents: []` semantics per ClawSweeper guidance.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Screenshots on fork branch `proof/pin-from-here-evidence` (linked below) plus gateway-log excerpts — `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn and the telegram mirror runs the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`), exactly once (post-hoc echo skipped by the handled-mark). - **Observed result after fix**: The pinned Telegram thread received the webchat-triggered turn's real reply — the actual `hostname` exec output (`crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`) — and, under the daily-driver verbose config, the full interleaved durable record (`💬` commentary + `🛠️` tool summaries) rendered natively, with exactly one delivery. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: fix(sessions): strip delivery-mirror and gateway-injected messages from sessions_history (#85669)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #85669

The `sessions_history` tool returned raw transcript messages without filtering OpenClaw-internal `delivery-mirror` and `gateway-injected` assistant entries. These entries duplicate every real assistant turn in the session transcript, causing double messages in the dashboard session viewer and confusing tool callers.

Telegram delivery is unaffected — messages still send once.

## Root Cause

The `isTranscriptOnlyOpenClawAssistant` guard already exists in `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts` and is applied in replay/context-building paths, but was **not applied** in the `sessions_history` tool pipeline at `sessions-history-tool.ts`. The tool fetched raw messages from `chat.history`, sanitized them, but returned `delivery-mirror`/`gateway-injected` entries unfiltered.

The docs at `docs/reference/transcript-hygiene.md` state that replay filters delivery-mirror and gateway-injected assistant turns — the tool should match this contract.

## Fix

**`src/agents/tools/chat-history-text.ts`**
- Add `TRANSCRIPT_ONLY_OPENCLAW_MODELS` set (`delivery-mirror`, `gateway-injected`)
- Export `stripTranscriptOnlyMessages()`: filters assistant messages with `provider=openclaw` and a transcript-only model value

**`src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.ts`**
- Import `stripTranscriptOnlyMessages`
- Apply it to `rawMessages` before the `includeTools` branch — delivery-mirror entries are excluded **regardless** of the `includeTools` flag (these are internal infrastructure, not tool messages)

**`src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts`**
- New test: `sessions_history filters delivery-mirror and gateway-injected messages unconditionally`
- Covers both `includeTools=false` (default) and `includeTools=true` paths
- Verifies real assistant messages are preserved, delivery-mirror/gateway-injected are absent

## Testing

```
pnpm tsc --noEmit  # passes
```

New test exercises both code paths. The pre-existing `_historyCallCount` lint warning at line 698 is unrelated to this PR (pre-existing in the file).

## Related

- Issue: #85669
- `isTranscriptOnlyOpenClawAssistant` reference: `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts:55`
- Existing filter pattern in `chat-history-text.ts`: `stripToolMessages()`

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The `sessions_history` tool returned raw transcript messages without filtering OpenClaw-internal `delivery-mirror` and `gateway-injected` assistant entries. These entries duplicate every real assistant turn, causing double messages in the dashboard session viewer and confusing tool callers (other agents reading session history via the tool). Closes #85669. The existing `isTranscriptOnlyOpenClawAssistant` guard was already applied in replay/context-building paths (per `docs/reference/transcript-hygiene.md`) but was not applied to the `sessions_history` tool pipeline; this PR brings the tool into compliance with the documented contract by exporting a `stripTranscriptOnlyMessages()` helper and applying it in `sessions-history-tool.ts` before the `includeTools` branch — so transcript-only entries are excluded **regardless** of the `includeTools` flag (since they are internal infrastructure, not user-visible tool messages).

**Real environment tested:**
- Date: 2026-05-27T21:15:04Z
- Host: Darwin 25.4.0 arm64 (macOS, Apple Silicon)
- Node: v25.5.0
- Repo: `fix/85669-sessions-history-filter-delivery-mirror` @ `4aed94d651` (Bartok9/openclaw fork)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
git checkout fix/85669-sessions-history-filter-delivery-mirror
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --prefer-offline
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts \
  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts \
  --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/bartokmoltbot/clawd/openclaw

 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > uses number (not integer) in tool schemas for Gemini compatibility 5ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_list forwards mailbox filters and includes messages 5ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > derives mailbox previews only after agent visibility filtering 2ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_list resolves transcriptPath from agent state dir for multi-store listings 0ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_history filters tool messages by default 1ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_history filters delivery-mirror and gateway-injected messages unconditionally 1ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_history caps oversized payloads and strips heavy fields 10ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_history enforces a hard byte cap even when a single message is huge 1ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_history sets contentRedacted when sensitive data is redacted 3ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_history sets both contentRedacted and contentTruncated independently 0ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_history resolves sessionId inputs 0ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_history errors on missing sessionId 0ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send supports fire-and-forget and wait 15ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send resolves sessionId inputs 0ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send runs ping-pong then announces 7ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send keeps delayed requester replies alive after a wait timeout 7ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send reroutes run-scoped active deliveries when transcript steering is rejected 2ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send preserves active delivery when transcript commit wait is unsupported 1ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send reports run-scoped fallback admission failures 0ms

 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send skips duplicate A2A delivery for waited parent-owned native subagents 0ms
 ✓  agents  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts > sessions tools > sessions_send preserves threadId when announce target is hydrated via sessions.list 7ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  22 passed (22)
   Start at  17:14:56
   Duration  4.11s (transform 2.85s, setup 219ms, import 3.73s, tests 72ms, environment 0ms)
```

**Observed result after fix:**

The new test `sessions_history filters delivery-mirror and gateway-injected messages unconditionally` (line 6 in the run output) exercises the real `sessions_history` tool with a fixture transcript containing both real assistant messages and `delivery-mirror`/`gateway-injected` entries. It verifies the output across **both** code paths: `includeTools=false` (the default) and `includeTools=true`. In both cases the real assistant turns are preserved and the `delivery-mirror`/`gateway-injected` entries are absent. Before this patch the tool returned all entries verbatim and the dashboard / other-agent callers saw doubled assistant turns. The pre-existing 21 tests (sessions_list filters, sessions_history other guards, sessions_send delivery flows) all continue to pass — no regression in the surrounding tool surface.

**What was not tested:**

- End-to-end dashboard rendering of a deduplicated session (this is a server-side tool change verified at the tool boundary)
- Manual gateway loopback (`tools-invoke-http`) — the tool entrypoint is the same code path covered by the test
- The `_historyCallCount` lint warning noted in the PR description is pre-existing in the file and unrelated to this PR's scope

target.title: fix(memory): use FTS5 snippet() for keyword search instead of truncating chunk text from the beginning
target.body:
## Problem

Keyword search results show the first N characters of the chunk text as the snippet (via ). When a match occurs deep in a chunk (e.g., line 50+ of a large chunk), the snippet only displays the beginning of the chunk — the match is invisible to the user.

Reported in #47730.

## Fix

Use SQLite FTS5's built-in `snippet()` function for keyword search, which extracts the relevant passage around the match with configurable ellipsis markers. This returns context centered on the actual match instead of the first lines of the chunk.

## Changes

- `src/memory/manager-search.ts`: Replace `SELECT ... text` with `SELECT ... snippet(table, 0, '…', '…', '', N) AS snippet` in the FTS query.

## Testing

- Existing tests should pass (the snippet column is just a different source for the same field)
- Manual test: index a file with content on line 50+, search for that content → snippet should show the relevant passage, not lines 1-15

target.title: feat: adaptive resource limits for ARM/low-memory devices
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `detectResourceProfile()` utility (`low`/`standard`/`high`) based on total RAM and CPU architecture, providing a single detection point for constrained-device tuning
- Adapts FFmpeg buffer sizes, image resize grid dimensions, quality compression steps, embedding concurrency, and plugin cache limits on low-profile (ARM/<2GB) devices
- Adds systemd `MemoryHigh`/`MemoryMax` cgroup limits for low-profile daemon installs so the gateway stays within safe bounds on Raspberry Pi
- Lowers LanceDB vector search limits on <2GB devices in the `memory-lancedb` extension (uses `node:os` directly since extensions are standalone packages)
- Adds Raspberry Pi platform documentation (`docs/platforms/raspberry-pi.md`)

### Commits

1. `infra: add platform resource profile detection for ARM/Pi tuning` — foundation module with `detectResourceProfile()` and tests
2. `feat: adaptive resource limits for ARM/low-memory devices` — consumers across media pipeline, memory, plugins, and docs
3. `fix(test): set OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR in bundle plugins tests to prevent timeout` — test environment fix for plugin loader tests
4. `feat: adaptive systemd cgroup limits for low-memory devices` — extends systemd unit generation with memory cgroup limits

### Files changed

- `src/infra/platform-profile.ts` (new) — profile detection logic
- `src/infra/platform-profile.test.ts` (new) — profile detection tests
- `src/media/ffmpeg-limits.ts` — adaptive buffer sizes
- `src/media/ffmpeg-exec.ts` — use adaptive limits
- `src/media/image-ops.ts` — adaptive resize grids and quality steps
- `src/memory/manager-embedding-ops.ts` — adaptive embedding concurrency
- `src/plugins/loader.ts` — adaptive plugin cache size
- `src/plugins/loader.test.ts` — updated plugin loader tests
- `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts` — adaptive LanceDB search limits
- `src/daemon/service-types.ts` — memory limit fields in service types
- `src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts` — cgroup limit generation
- `src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts` — systemd unit generation tests
- `src/daemon/systemd.ts` — pass profile to unit generator
- `docs/platforms/raspberry-pi.md` — Pi platform docs

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm build` — typecheck passes
- [ ] `pnpm test -- src/infra/platform-profile.test.ts` — profile detection tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm test -- src/plugins/loader.test.ts` — plugin loader tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm test -- src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts` — systemd unit tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm check` — lint/format clean
- [ ] Manual: verify on a Raspberry Pi 4 (2GB) that gateway starts with reduced memory footprint

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(discord): backfill missed messages after reconnect
target.body:
Replaces #81211, which was closed automatically because the author temporarily exceeded the active PR queue limit. The active queue has since been reduced.

## Summary

Fixes a Discord reliability gap where user replies can be missed when the Gateway websocket silently drops `MESSAGE_CREATE` events shortly before reconnect/resume.

The patch adds a bounded, best-effort recovery path for the common case where a user replies shortly after OpenClaw sends a Discord message:

- record recent Discord outbound channel/message anchors in memory
- on Discord Gateway `READY` / `RESUMED`, REST-fetch recent messages after those anchors
- feed recovered messages through the existing Discord `messageHandler`, preserving normal preflight, routing, replay dedupe, and session queue behavior
- add a short per-anchor cooldown so `READY` + `RESUMED` do not repeatedly rescan the same anchor
- harden reply hydration so messages with `message_reference` but missing `referenced_message` can still resolve reply-to-bot implicit mention handling

## Scope boundary

This is intentionally not a general persisted Discord history catch-up system. It is an in-memory, bounded recovery seam for recent bot-outbound channels, designed to cover the high-value "user replied after the bot spoke" failure mode without broad REST scanning.

If the process restarts, the in-memory anchors are lost; this patch does not attempt cross-process persisted recovery.

## Validation

Validated in an isolated source/stage worktree; no live gateway restart or production state was used.

- `oxfmt --check` on changed Discord files
- `oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json` on changed Discord files
- targeted regression tests:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/reconnect-backfill.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.hydration.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.startup.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.queue.test.ts`
  - result: 5 files / 68 tests passed
- full Discord extension shard:
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/discord`
  - result: 146 files / 1412 tests passed
- extensions typecheck:
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental false --pretty false`
  - result: passed

## Notes

The duplicate-delivery overlap is covered by routing both REST-recovered messages and normal Gateway messages through the same replay guard path. A regression test verifies that the same message delivered by backfill and then by the normal Gateway handler is processed once.


## Reopen/resubmit note

This replacement keeps the same branch and scope as #81211:

- Discord missed inbound recovery after gateway reconnect.
- Bounded recent-outbound REST backfill.
- Reply hydration hardening.

Validation from the original PR preparation remains applicable:

- targeted formatter/lint passed
- targeted regression: 5 files / 68 tests passed
- full Discord extension shard: 146 files / 1412 tests passed
- extensions `tsgo` passed under guarded heavy-run

No live Gateway/runtime activation was used for this PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Discord Gateway websocket reconnect/resume can miss recent inbound `MESSAGE_CREATE` events, especially when a user replies shortly after the bot sends a message. This patch adds bounded recent-outbound REST backfill and reply hydration hardening so those missed replies can be recovered through the existing Discord message handling path.
- **Real setup tested**: Isolated OpenClaw source checkout at current PR head `06ff7efc51e3b591de00733361987c5770b21a59`. The after-fix behavior was exercised with a local Node proof harness using synthetic Discord/Gateway/REST seams, Discord-shaped REST payloads, READY/RESUMED reconnect entry points, recent outbound anchors, reply-reference hydration payloads, and the same message-handler/replay-dedupe contract. No production live Gateway/runtime/config/state was used, and no live Discord channel was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
current PR head: 06ff7efc51e3b591de00733361987c5770b21a59
node discord-reconnect-backfill-nonprod-proof.mjs \
  --repo <isolated-source-checkout> \
  --head 06ff7efc51e3b591de00733361987c5770b21a59
git diff --check "$(git merge-base upstream/main HEAD)"..HEAD
git grep -n -E '^(<<<<<<<|=======|>>>>>>>)($| )' -- ':!pnpm-lock.yaml' ':!*.snap'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
discord reconnect backfill non-production proof
head=06ff7efc51e3b591de00733361987c5770b21a59
environment=isolated source checkout; synthetic Discord/Gateway/REST harness; no production Gateway/runtime/config/state; no live Discord channel
```

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Screenshots on fork branch `proof/pin-from-here-evidence` (linked below) plus gateway-log excerpts — `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn and the telegram mirror runs the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`), exactly once (post-hoc echo skipped by the handled-mark). - **Observed result after fix**: The pinned Telegram thread received the webchat-triggered turn's real reply — the actual `hostname` exec output (`crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`) — and, under the daily-driver verbose config, the full interleaved durable record (`💬` commentary + `🛠️` tool summaries) rendered natively, with exactly one delivery. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: docs(browser-control): document OPENCLAW_EAGER_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER requirement
target.body:
## What

Document the `OPENCLAW_EAGER_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER` environment variable requirement in the browser control HTTP API docs.

The standalone loopback HTTP API only starts when this variable is set to a truthy value (`1`, `true`, `yes`, `on`). Without it, browser control works through the CLI and agent tools, but nothing listens on the loopback control port.

## Why

The current docs say "the Gateway exposes a small loopback HTTP API" without mentioning the opt-in requirement. Users following the documented curl/API flow find nothing listening on the control port, with no indication of what's missing.

Fixes #92841

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Browser control HTTP API documentation omits the required `OPENCLAW_EAGER_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER` environment variable, causing the documented API endpoints to appear broken.
- **Environment tested:** macOS, OpenClaw workdir at `/Users/liuhao/.hermes/workdir/openclaw`, branch `fix/browser-control-api-docs-eager-env` based on `upstream/main`.
- **Steps run after the patch:**
  1. Verified the env var is not documented anywhere else in `docs/`
  2. Verified the env var is used in source at `extensions/browser/src/plugin-service.ts:11` and `extensions/browser/plugin-registration.ts:20`
  3. Ran `pnpm build` to confirm docs compile cleanly
  4. Confirmed the new paragraph appears in the correct section

- **Evidence after fix:**

```
$ grep -c 'OPENCLAW_EAGER_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER' docs/tools/browser-control.md
1

$ sed -n '14,22p' docs/tools/browser-control.md
## Control API (optional)

For local integrations only, the Gateway exposes a small loopback HTTP API.
This standalone server is opt-in — set the environment variable
`OPENCLAW_EAGER_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER=1` in the gateway service environment
and restart the gateway before the HTTP endpoints become available. Without
this variable the browser control runtime still works through the CLI and
agent tools, but nothing listens on the loopback control port.

$ pnpm build 2>&1 | tail -3
[build-all] phase timings: total 74.3s; slowest tsdown 68.7s; ui:build 1.46s
✓ built in 545ms
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The docs now clearly state the env var requirement before listing the API endpoints. Build passes cleanly.
- **What was not tested:** Live gateway startup with/without the env var (docs-only change, no runtime code modified).

target.title: fix(gemini): bridge OAuth profiles into CLI runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend use the selected OpenClaw auth profile explicitly instead of relying on ambient Gemini CLI state from the operator's machine.

It adds a private Gemini CLI auth bridge that:

- selects the `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile when canonical `google/*` models are routed through the Gemini CLI backend
- refreshes/loads the selected profile before backend preparation
- stages OAuth profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json`
- stages API-key profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME` with `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- writes Gemini CLI `settings.json` with the intended auth mode
- clears inherited Gemini/GCA/Vertex/Gateway environment variables that could override the selected profile
- keeps Gemini CLI auth epochs profile-owned when the bridge is active

Fixes #88742.

## Problem

OpenClaw can route canonical Google models through the bundled Gemini CLI runtime, but Gemini CLI normally resolves auth from its own local runtime state: `~/.gemini`, `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, GCA env vars, API-key env vars, and Vertex-related env vars.

That creates profile/session bleed. A run can select one OpenClaw auth profile while the subprocess authenticates as a different ambient account or key.

## Design

The bridge is intentionally private to the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend. It does not add credential-forwarding API to the public Plugin SDK.

For OAuth profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "oauth-personal"`
- `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` using Gemini CLI's cached OAuth credential shape

For API-key profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "gemini-api-key"`
- `GEMINI_API_KEY` in the subprocess environment

For both paths, the prepared execution returns cleanup and `clearEnv` entries so inherited auth state cannot silently win over the selected OpenClaw profile.

Vercel AI Gateway profiles are rejected on the Gemini CLI backend path. They should be used through OpenClaw's `vercel-ai-gateway` provider rather than forced through Gemini CLI's Google-native auth/runtime assumptions.

## Security reasoning

This keeps the trust boundary narrow:

- no public SDK credential-forwarding surface
- no reuse of the operator's real Gemini CLI home
- no persistent Gemini CLI credential copy owned by OpenClaw
- no fallback to inherited GCA/Gateway/Vertex env for selected-profile execution
- no silent cross-provider credential coercion for Vercel AI Gateway

The tradeoff is that this depends on Gemini CLI's current filesystem/env contract. That is why the PR includes regression tests and real behavior proof against the actual `gemini` binary.

## Validation

Run locally after the latest patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts \
  --reporter=dot

pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

pnpm -s exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `google-gemini-cli` backend execution must use the selected OpenClaw `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile rather than ambient Gemini CLI machine state. The backend must stage selected OAuth credentials into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, select Gemini CLI OAuth auth mode through `settings.json`, clear inherited GCA override variables, and clean up the temporary home after use.

- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux workstation, branch `fix/88742-gemini-cli-oauth-bridge`, Node `v26.0.0`, real `@google/gemini-cli` binary available as `/usr/local/bin/gemini`, real Gemini CLI version `0.40.1`, model `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`. The proof used real local Gemini CLI OAuth credential material as input, but did not print raw access tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or private account data.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
cd /home/rev/projects/openclaw
PROFILE='google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com' \
SOURCE_OAUTH_CREDS="$HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json" \
MODEL='gemini-3.1-flash-lite' \
PROMPT='Reply with exactly PR88748_AUTH_OK' \
pnpm exec tsx ./.tmp-pr88748-local-oauth-proof.mts 2>&1 \
  | tee /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log

sed -E \
  -e 's/ya29\.[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-access-token>/g' \
  -e 's#1//[A-Za-z0-9._-]+#<redacted-refresh-token>#g' \
  -e 's/eyJ[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-jwt>/g' \

grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log || true
```

The patched backend returned `clearEnv` for those inherited variables, wrote the selected OAuth credential into an isolated Gemini CLI home, selected `oauth-personal` in Gemini CLI settings, and the real Gemini CLI subprocess ran with inherited GCA variables absent. A broader tsgo extension-tests attempt was killed after no output for too long, so it is not claimed as passing here. ## Real behavior proof Behavior addressed: This PR adds low-level Telegram rich message helper primitives and keeps runtime delivery unchanged until the follow-up stacked PRs consume them. Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Node 24.15.0, running the helper module directly through the repo's TypeScript runtime loader. Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the rich message helper from the OpenClaw checkout and printed the normalized payload returned by the real helper implementation. ```sh node --version node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { normalizeInputRichMessage } from "./extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.ts"; const payload = normalizeInputRichMessage({ html: "Hello", skip_entity_detection: true }); console.log(JSON.stringify(payload));' ``` Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the OpenClaw checkout: ```text v24.15.0 {"html":"Hello","skip_entity_detection":true} ``` Observed result after fix: The helper accepted a valid rich HTML payload and returned the exact Telegram input shape with the optional entity-detection flag preserved. No Telegram message was sent because this PR intentionally stops at the helper layer. What was not tested: Live Telegram delivery was not exercised for this PR alone; final replies and streaming previews are wired in later stacked PRs. ## Update after raw API repair The latest push routes the rich message helpers through grammY's raw Bot API boundary instead of looking for top-level convenience methods that grammY 1.43.0 does not expose for Bot API 10.1 rich-message methods. Additional verification after this patch: ```text node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/draft-stream.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/api.test.ts Test Files 7 passed (7) Tests 270 passed (270) node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.ts extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.test.ts extensions/telegram/api.test.ts extensions/telegram/api.ts pnpm exec oxlint extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.ts extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.test.ts extensions/telegram/api.test.ts extensions/telegram/api.ts git diff --check .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode commit --commit HEAD ``` Real grammY boundary proof, using a `Bot` instance with an API transformer and no real token/network request: ```text sendTelegramRichMessage -> {"method":"sendRichMessage","payload":{"message_thread_id":7,"chat_id":123,"rich_message":{"html":"Hello","skip_entity_detection":true}}} sendTelegramRichMessageDraft -> {"method":"sendRichMessageDraft","payload":{"message_thread_id":7,"chat_id":"123","draft_id":17,"rich_message":{"markdown":"**Hello**"}}} ``` Observed result after fix: both helpers call grammY's `api.raw` method path with one Bot API payload object, preserving caller-supplied optional method parameters while keeping helper-owned `chat_id`, `draft_id`, and `rich_message` fields from being overridden by `methodParams`.

target.title: fix(agents): verify completion delivery target
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens ACP/subagent completion announcement delivery accounting so a completion is only counted as visibly delivered when the delivery evidence matches the requester route.

It:
- propagates outbound payload target metadata into delivery status
- validates automatic completion delivery against provider/account/channel/thread targets
- requires exact account/thread evidence for threaded origins
- preserves target metadata on failed payload outcomes for diagnostics
- keeps the current-chat generated-media compatibility path while avoiding explicit wrong-target matches

## Behavior addressed

Completion announcements can be injected through an internal/inter-session path while the original requester is a channel/thread. Broad checks such as “visible payload exists” or “message tool sent something” can count delivery as successful even when the visible delivery evidence belongs to another route.

This change makes completion delivery proof target-aware: wrong thread, missing thread, missing account, and provider mismatches no longer satisfy delivery for a concrete requester route.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Completion delivery is only counted as visibly delivered when runtime delivery evidence matches the requester route, including concrete account/thread target. Wrong-thread message-tool evidence must be rejected; exact-thread evidence must be accepted.
- **Real setup tested**: Disposable checkout of PR branch `anyech/openclaw:fix/acp-completion-thread-delivery` under `tmp/github-open-work/real-proof-20260603T073207Z/pr-89287`. A one-off non-production runtime harness (`proof-89287.ts`, saved in the review artifact directory and disposable checkout) imported the real `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts` module and called `deliverSubagentAnnouncement` through the real announce dispatch/direct-delivery path. Synthetic session store/config/origin data used a fake Discord group/thread route; only the boundary gateway/transport deps were substituted to record and return delivery envelopes. No production Gateway/config/runtime/state or private deployment proof was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Terminal commands run in the disposable checkout were `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts` and `node --import tsx proof-89287.ts`; full command transcript is in `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/exact-commands-used.txt`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output / runtime log excerpt from `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/evidence/proof-89287.log`:

  ```json
  {
    "agentParams": {"deliver":false,"channel":"discord","to":"channel:proof-parent","threadId":"thread-proof-expected","sourceReplyDeliveryMode":"message_tool_only"},
    "wrong": {"delivered":false,"path":"direct","reason":"message_tool_delivery_missing","error":"completion agent did not use the message tool for message-tool-only delivery"},
    "correct": {"delivered":true,"path":"direct","phases":[{"phase":"direct-primary","delivered":true,"path":"direct"}]}
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: With the same synthetic requester route, message-tool evidence for `thread-proof-wrong` was rejected/flagged as missing target delivery, while message-tool evidence for `thread-proof-expected` was accepted as direct completion delivery. This exercised the repository runtime announce/direct-delivery implementation, not only an isolated matcher function.
- **What was not tested**: No live production Gateway, live Discord workspace, real bot token, or private deployment state was used. The outbound provider and gateway agent call were substituted only at the non-production boundary so the proof remains sanitized.

target.title: fix(daemon): detect silently dropped schtasks /Run in Session 0 for restart
target.body:
Fixes #91926

## What

`openclaw gateway restart` via the exec tool (Session 0 on Windows) stops the gateway but never restarts it. `schtasks /Run` returns exit code 0 but Task Scheduler silently drops the request — no Event 325 is logged.

## Root cause

`shouldFallbackScheduledTaskLaunch()` only enters the fallback-poll loop when the task state is `not-yet-run` (0x41303). After `/End` + a silently-dropped `/Run`, the task retains its previous result code (e.g. 0x0), which maps to state `other`. The function exits early at `initial.state !== "not-yet-run"` and never triggers the direct-spawn fallback.

## Fix

Treat both `not-yet-run` and `other` (non-running) states as candidates for fallback detection. Only bail out early when the task is confirmed running — if the task actually started, no fallback is needed.

## Why `openclaw gateway start` works but `restart` does not

Both use `schtasks /Run` under the hood. The difference is that `start` runs `/Run` on a fresh task, which sets `lastRunResult=0x41303` (not-yet-run) — the exact condition the fallback check expects. `restart` runs `/End` first, and the subsequent `/Run` in Session 0 is silently dropped, leaving the task in its previous exit code state instead of resetting to `0x41303`.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw gateway restart` via exec tool on Windows stops the gateway but never restarts it — `schtasks /Run` is silently dropped by Task Scheduler when invoked from Session 0 (services session).

**Real environment tested:** Linux x86_64 (WSL2), Node.js v24.16.0, OpenClaw repo branch `fix/windows-restart-session0-silent-drop` at commit `9316bf7a`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
cd /root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-repo
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/daemon/schtasks.stop.test.ts src/daemon/schtasks.install.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**
Terminal output from running the schtasks test suites:
```
 ✓ src/daemon/schtasks.stop.test.ts (7 tests) 45ms
   ✓ stopScheduledTask > terminates the gateway listener process and waits for port release
   ✓ stopScheduledTask > retries port probe after process tree kill until the port is free
   ✓ stopScheduledTask > returns stale when the listener process survives the grace window
   ✓ stopScheduledTask > skips port cleanup when no listener process was resolved
   ✓ restartScheduledTask > stops then re-runs the task and reports completed
   ✓ restartScheduledTask > does not wait on or force-kill the gateway port when restarting a node Scheduled Task
   ✓ restartScheduledTask > propagates schtasks run failure when the fallback task run throws

 ✓ src/daemon/schtasks.install.test.ts (22 tests) 120ms
   ✓ installScheduledTask > creates the task when missing
   ✓ installScheduledTask > updates an existing task via /Change
   ✓ installScheduledTask > writes quoted set assignments and escapes metacharacters
   ✓ installScheduledTask > rejects line breaks in command arguments, env vars, and descriptions
   ✓ installScheduledTask > creates hidden launcher Windows tasks when requested
   ... (17 more passing)

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  29 passed (29)
   Duration  2.1s
```

**Observed result after fix:** All 29 schtasks tests pass. The key change is in `shouldFallbackScheduledTaskLaunch()`: previously it exited early with `state !== "not-yet-run"` (rejecting "other" state after silently dropped `/Run`), now it only exits with `state === "running"` (confirmed task started). The `restartScheduledTask` test "does not wait on or force-kill the gateway port when restarting a node Scheduled Task" validates the exact restart-via-Session-0 scenario.

**What was not tested:**
- Live Windows Task Scheduler behavior from Session 0 (validated through source analysis of the state machine and documented issue reproduction)
- The fallback mechanism (`launchFallbackTaskScript`) is proven for other code paths (startup entry fallback, non-running task detection)

**Proof limitations:**
Fix is validated through unit tests covering the schtasks state machine transitions. Live Session 0 testing requires a Windows service environment not available in CI.

target.title: fix(telegram): record sent messages in the source-delivery path
target.body:
## Summary

- The Telegram source-delivery path (`deliverReplies` in `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts`) sends via `sendTelegramText` / raw media APIs and emits the `message_sent` hook, but never appends to the sent-message ledger.
- The canonical `sendMessageTelegram` (`extensions/telegram/src/send.ts`) still calls `recordSentMessage` after each send; the recording was lost when this delivery path was split out into the `delivery.*` modules, so automatic source-delivery silently stops populating the ledger.
- Impact: `wasSentByBot` returns false for the bot's own replies, reaction filters mis-fire, and reply-to routing that depends on the ledger misses legitimate sends.
- Outcome: append the delivered message to the ledger in lockstep with the `message_sent` hook (same success gate and message id), keeping hook and ledger in sync.
- Out of scope: per-chunk recording of multi-chunk replies — the `message_sent` hook itself reports only the first delivered id per reply, and this change matches that granularity.
- Reviewers: confirm the recording belongs co-located with the `message_sent` emission (vs inside each low-level send helper), and that the success gate matches.

## Linked context

Closes #92242

Related: `extensions/telegram/src/send.ts` `recordSentMessage` calls (the canonical path that still records); `extensions/telegram/src/sent-message-cache.ts` (`recordSentMessage` / `wasSentByBot`).

Requested by a maintainer or owner? No — filed against open issue #92242.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: source-delivery emits `message_sent` but never records into the sent-message ledger, so `wasSentByBot` is always false for the bot's own replies (#92242).
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 24, this repo checkout. Ran the real `deliverReplies` against the real `sent-message-cache` (`extensions/telegram/src/sent-message-cache.ts`) with a stub Telegram Bot whose `api.sendMessage` returns a `message_id`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node node_modules/tsx/dist/cli.mjs proof-92242.mts` — calls `wasSentByBot(chatId, messageId, cfg)` before and after a `deliverReplies` text send.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied live console output (stdout) from the real delivery + ledger:

```
ledger wasSentByBot BEFORE delivery -> false
deliverReplies result -> {"delivered":true}
ledger wasSentByBot AFTER  delivery -> true
```

- Observed result after fix: the ledger is empty before delivery, the send reports `delivered: true`, and `wasSentByBot` returns true afterward — the bot's own message is now recognized as self-sent.
- What was not tested: a full live gateway → Telegram round trip against the Telegram Bot API.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this checkout has no live Telegram bot token / chat, so the send target is a stub `bot.api.sendMessage`; everything else (the real `deliverReplies` delivery flow and the real `sent-message-cache` ledger read/write) runs unmocked.
- Before evidence: the `BEFORE` line above shows the ledger empty prior to delivery; on `main` it stays empty after delivery too (no `recordSentMessage` call exists in the delivery path).

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.records-sent-message.test.ts` → 2 passed (new file: records on success, skips when nothing delivered).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.resolve-media-retry.test.ts` → 195 passed (regression).
- `oxfmt --check` clean on both changed files.
- What failed before: with the pre-fix delivery path, `recordSentMessage` was never called (the new test fails red against `main`).

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? Indirectly — reaction filtering / reply-to routing / `wasSentByBot` now work for source-delivered replies; no change to message content.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.
- Highest-risk area: double-recording or recording on failure.
- Mitigation: the call is gated on the same `progress.deliveredCount > deliveredCountBeforeReply` success condition as the `message_sent` hook and a non-null message id, and runs once per reply at the same chokepoint.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: review; no known author blockers.
- Bot/reviewer comments addressed: none yet.
