target.title: fix(gemini): bridge OAuth profiles into CLI runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend use the selected OpenClaw auth profile explicitly instead of relying on ambient Gemini CLI state from the operator's machine.

It adds a private Gemini CLI auth bridge that:

- selects the `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile when canonical `google/*` models are routed through the Gemini CLI backend
- refreshes/loads the selected profile before backend preparation
- stages OAuth profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json`
- stages API-key profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME` with `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- writes Gemini CLI `settings.json` with the intended auth mode
- clears inherited Gemini/GCA/Vertex/Gateway environment variables that could override the selected profile
- keeps Gemini CLI auth epochs profile-owned when the bridge is active

Fixes #88742.

## Problem

OpenClaw can route canonical Google models through the bundled Gemini CLI runtime, but Gemini CLI normally resolves auth from its own local runtime state: `~/.gemini`, `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, GCA env vars, API-key env vars, and Vertex-related env vars.

That creates profile/session bleed. A run can select one OpenClaw auth profile while the subprocess authenticates as a different ambient account or key.

## Design

The bridge is intentionally private to the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend. It does not add credential-forwarding API to the public Plugin SDK.

For OAuth profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "oauth-personal"`
- `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` using Gemini CLI's cached OAuth credential shape

For API-key profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "gemini-api-key"`
- `GEMINI_API_KEY` in the subprocess environment

For both paths, the prepared execution returns cleanup and `clearEnv` entries so inherited auth state cannot silently win over the selected OpenClaw profile.

Vercel AI Gateway profiles are rejected on the Gemini CLI backend path. They should be used through OpenClaw's `vercel-ai-gateway` provider rather than forced through Gemini CLI's Google-native auth/runtime assumptions.

## Security reasoning

This keeps the trust boundary narrow:

- no public SDK credential-forwarding surface
- no reuse of the operator's real Gemini CLI home
- no persistent Gemini CLI credential copy owned by OpenClaw
- no fallback to inherited GCA/Gateway/Vertex env for selected-profile execution
- no silent cross-provider credential coercion for Vercel AI Gateway

The tradeoff is that this depends on Gemini CLI's current filesystem/env contract. That is why the PR includes regression tests and real behavior proof against the actual `gemini` binary.

## Validation

Run locally after the latest patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts \
  --reporter=dot

pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

pnpm -s exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `google-gemini-cli` backend execution must use the selected OpenClaw `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile rather than ambient Gemini CLI machine state. The backend must stage selected OAuth credentials into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, select Gemini CLI OAuth auth mode through `settings.json`, clear inherited GCA override variables, and clean up the temporary home after use.

- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux workstation, branch `fix/88742-gemini-cli-oauth-bridge`, Node `v26.0.0`, real `@google/gemini-cli` binary available as `/usr/local/bin/gemini`, real Gemini CLI version `0.40.1`, model `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`. The proof used real local Gemini CLI OAuth credential material as input, but did not print raw access tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or private account data.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
cd /home/rev/projects/openclaw
PROFILE='google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com' \
SOURCE_OAUTH_CREDS="$HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json" \
MODEL='gemini-3.1-flash-lite' \
PROMPT='Reply with exactly PR88748_AUTH_OK' \
pnpm exec tsx ./.tmp-pr88748-local-oauth-proof.mts 2>&1 \
  | tee /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log

sed -E \
  -e 's/ya29\.[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-access-token>/g' \
  -e 's#1//[A-Za-z0-9._-]+#<redacted-refresh-token>#g' \
  -e 's/eyJ[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-jwt>/g' \
  -e 's/AIza[[:alnum:]_-]+/<redacted-google-api-key>/g' \
  /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log \
  > /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log

grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log || true
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
=== PR 88748 local Gemini CLI OAuth proof ===
profile=google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com
model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite
inherited_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA=true
inherited_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN_present=true
source_provider=google-gemini-cli
source_has_access=true
source_has_refresh=true
source_has_id_token=true
source_expired=false
prepared_GEMINI_CLI_HOME=/tmp/openclaw/gemini-cli-home-iEQBYF
prepared_clearEnv=["GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA","GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN"]
clears_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA=true
clears_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN=true
oauth_creds_shape={"type":"authorized_user","has_access_token":true,"has_refresh_token":true,"has_expiry_date":true,"token_type":"Bearer","has_id_token":true}
settings={"security":{"auth":{"selectedType":"oauth-personal"}}}
child_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA_present=false
child_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN_present=false
real_gemini_exit=0
real_gemini_signal=
real_gemini_stdout_begin
{
  "session_id": "071a4f5c-f530-4242-9a9a-e1618c8abfc9",
  "response": "PR88748_AUTH_OK",
  "stats": {
    "models": {
      "gemini-3.1-flash-lite": {
        "api": {
          "totalRequests": 1,
          "totalErrors": 0,
          "totalLatencyMs": 1459
        },
        "tokens": {
          "input": 13057,
          "prompt": 13057,
          "candidates": 10,
          "total": 13067,
          "cached": 0,
          "thoughts": 0,
          "tool": 0
        }
      }
    },
    "tools": {
      "totalCalls": 0,
      "totalSuccess": 0,
      "totalFail": 0
    }
  }
}
real_gemini_stdout_end
real_gemini_stderr_begin
Ripgrep is not available. Falling back to GrepTool.
real_gemini_stderr_end
cleanup_removed_home=true
proof_result=PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The parent process had conflicting inherited GCA environment variables set. The patched backend returned `clearEnv` for those inherited variables, wrote the selected OAuth credential into an isolated Gemini CLI home, selected `oauth-personal` in Gemini CLI settings, and the real Gemini CLI subprocess ran with inherited GCA variables absent. The prompt returned the expected `PR88748_AUTH_OK` marker with `real_gemini_exit=0`, and cleanup removed the isolated home.

- **What was not tested**: The real behavior proof above covers the live OAuth/GCA override path. The API-key path and Vercel rejection path are covered by source-level tests in this PR; a live API-key prompt proof can be added separately if required. The proof intentionally avoids printing raw OAuth tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or account-private credential material.

- **Sanitization check**: `grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log` produced no matches.

target.title: fix(agents): add usage guidance to sessions_spawn tool description (fixes #91814)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: The `sessions_spawn` tool description is purely a technical reference listing parameters and mechanics. It lacks usage guidance about *when* agents should delegate work to sub-agents, causing models to consistently bypass sub-agent delegation even for large/batch tasks.
- **Root Cause**: The tool description in `tool-description-presets.ts` only describes how the tool works mechanically, not when to use it. Models see the description at decision time but get no signal about delegation strategy.
- **Fix**: Add three concise usage-guidance lines to the `sessions_spawn` description following the same style as the existing entries: when to delegate, when not to, and what to do after spawning.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/tool-description-presets.ts` — added 3 lines to `describeSessionsSpawnTool()` base description array
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - The technical parameter descriptions (runtime, mode, context) remain unchanged
  - No behavioral changes to the sessions_spawn tool itself
  - Other tool descriptions are unaffected

### Reproduction

1. Ask any model (e.g. DeepSeek v4, Claude) to perform a large task like reading 10+ files or doing multi-step web searches
2. Observe that the model reads files one-by-one in the main session instead of delegating to sub-agents
3. **Before this PR**: The `sessions_spawn` description provides no delegation guidance — models default to local execution
4. **After this PR**: The description includes explicit delegation signals (when/when-not/after-spawn), encouraging sub-agent use for parallel/sidecar tasks

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (91814)**: The `sessions_spawn` tool description presented to models at tool-call decision time now includes usage guidance for delegation strategy.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22 — `pnpm build && pnpm check` confirmed the tool-description change compiles cleanly and passes type checking.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Verified the updated description string output by inspecting `describeSessionsSpawnTool()` return value.

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
The sessions_spawn description now includes:

"Delegate sidecar/parallel tasks: batch file reads, multi-step searches, data collection."
"Avoid delegating quick lookups, single-file reads, or tasks on your critical path."
"After spawning, do meaningful non-overlapping work; results arrive when complete."
```

These follow the same terse style as the existing description entries.

**Observed result after fix**: The tool description now communicates delegation strategy — when to use sub-agents (batch/parallel work), when to avoid them (quick lookups), and what to do after spawning (do other work). This aligns with the approach used by Codex's `spawn_agent` tool description.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end model behavior change was not measured — the fix improves the prompt engineering surface but actual model behavior depends on the specific model family and context.

**Repro confirmation**: Before the fix, the description ended at `"Use for fresh child-session work."` with no guidance on what constitutes appropriate child-session work. After the fix, agents receive clear delegation signals alongside the tool definition.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: More verbose tool descriptions could marginally increase prompt token count.
  **Mitigation**: Three short lines (~50 tokens) is negligible compared to the potential token savings from actual sub-agent delegation (avoiding repeated file reads in the main session).
- **Risk**: Usage guidance could bias models toward over-delegation.
  **Mitigation**: The "Avoid delegating" line explicitly constrains when NOT to use sub-agents, providing balanced guidance.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix (or more accurately: behavioral improvement / prompt-engineering fix)

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent tool descriptions / model-facing instructions

### Regression Test Plan

- `pnpm build && pnpm check` — the change is additive text only; no code logic altered.

### Review Findings Addressed

N/A (initial submission)

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #91814

target.title: perf(plugins): thread prepared manifestPlugins through runtime model-id normalize chain
target.body:
## Summary

Extends the prepared-manifest-metadata threading from #82814 into the runtime plugin-normalize branch so callers that already carry prepared `manifestPlugins` skip the residual `installs.json` slot-or-load disk walk in `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies`. Closes the gap that #82814's measurements left visible (Kova `agent-cold-warm-message` was at 28 metadata scans per cold+warm sample post-#82814).

Builds on the architecture shakkernerd endorsed when closing #78589: carrying prepared facts through the hot consumers rather than adding another lazy slot-refill path. Does not touch `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies` itself.

## Why

`#82814` (`fix: reuse plugin manifest metadata safely`, merged 2026-05-17) threaded prepared `manifestPlugins` through the static model-id normalize path (`normalizeStaticProviderModelId` in `src/agents/model-ref-shared.ts`) so consumers stop consulting the `current-plugin-metadata-snapshot` single-slot handoff in `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies`. That eliminated most of the hot-path `installs.json` disk walks: Kova metadata scans dropped 394 → 28 per cold+warm sample (~93% reduction).

The remaining 28 scans come from the runtime plugin-normalize branch. `src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts` already receives `options.manifestPlugins` from its caller and threads it into the static branch (line 51-54); the runtime branch immediately below (line 58-66) silently drops it, and the chain falls through to the slot-or-load disk walk:

\`\`\`
src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts:59
  → normalizeProviderModelIdWithRuntime(...)         (no plugins param)
    → normalizeProviderModelIdWithPlugin(params)     (no plugins param)
      → normalizeProviderModelIdWithManifest(params) (params.plugins undefined)
        → loadManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies   (slot-or-load disk walk)
\`\`\`

`normalizeProviderModelIdWithManifest` already honors `params.plugins` to short-circuit:

\`\`\`ts
function loadManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies(params) {
  if (params.plugins) {
    return collectManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies(params.plugins);  // prepared path
  }
  const { snapshot, cacheable } = resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies(params);  // slot-or-load
  // ...
}
\`\`\`

The intermediate functions just never accepted a `plugins` field. This PR adds it as an additive optional param and forwards `options?.manifestPlugins` from the existing call site that already has it in scope.

## How

Three surgical edits:

1. **`src/agents/provider-model-normalization.runtime.ts`** — add optional `plugins?: readonly Pick<PluginManifestRecord, \"modelIdNormalization\">[]` to `normalizeProviderModelIdWithRuntime`'s params. The body just forwards `params` unchanged to `normalizeProviderModelIdWithPlugin`, so no behavioral change beyond the type signature.

2. **`src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts`** — add the same optional `plugins?` field to `normalizeProviderModelIdWithPlugin`'s params. The existing `normalizeProviderModelIdWithManifest(params)` call already forwards everything, so `plugins` flows straight through to where it's honored.

3. **`src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts:59`** — at the runtime call site, thread `options?.manifestPlugins` into the runtime call. Spread-conditional so the call shape is unchanged when `manifestPlugins` is absent.

Net diff: +6 LOC across three source files (plus focused tests and the runtime proof script).

## Relationship to the previously closed PR #78589

`#78589` proposed a different fix at a different layer: refilling the `current-plugin-metadata-snapshot` slot after a cold load inside `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies`. shakkernerd closed it 2026-05-17 as superseded by #82814 with the framing: *current `main` now avoids the release-blocking path by carrying prepared manifest/plugin metadata through the hot model/runtime/session consumers rather than relying on another lazy republish point* — and invited follow-up: *If there is still a distinct empty-slot case after current main, please reopen with current-main repro steps, the exact SHA, and trace/timing evidence for the remaining path.*

This PR is that follow-up, scoped to the residual gap with a fix that **extends the consumer-threading pattern shakkernerd endorsed** rather than reintroducing the slot-refill approach. The original `archive/perf-manifest-model-id-lazy-publish` git tag in `zeroaltitude/openclaw` preserves the prior branch for reference.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Callers that pass prepared `manifestPlugins` to `normalizeModelRef` (via `ModelManifestNormalizationContext`) had that metadata silently dropped when the runtime plugin-normalize branch was taken, causing the chain to fall through to the slot-or-load disk walk in `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies` even though prepared metadata was available in the caller's scope.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `1c82080f6a` in the clean `perf/manifest-model-id-thread-runtime-plugins` worktree on June 13, 2026 MST, with the real `normalizeModelRef` -> `normalizeProviderModelIdWithRuntime` -> `normalizeProviderModelIdWithPlugin` -> `normalizeProviderModelIdWithManifest` chain. The proof ran against a fresh empty `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and instrumented the live SQLite installed-plugin-index store path returned by `resolveInstalledPluginIndexStorePath(...)` / `resolveOpenClawStateSqlitePath(...)`; no Vitest harness or mocks of the seam under test were used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-85254-manifest-plugins-runtime-thread.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-selection.plugin-runtime.test.ts src/agents/model-selection-manifest-workspace.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-model-id-normalization.test.ts`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
  - `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/provider-model-normalization.runtime.ts src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts src/agents/model-selection.plugin-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts scripts/proof-85254-manifest-plugins-runtime-thread.ts`
  - `git diff --check`
  - `pnpm build`
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live proof run on current head:

  ```text
  [1/3] without-prepared-plugins-walks-snapshot (control)
      normalizeModelRef result: provider=openai model=gpt-5
      sentinel disk touches on <stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite: 2
      touched paths: <stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite
      ok
  [2/3] with-prepared-plugins-consults-them-and-skips-snapshot
      normalizeModelRef result: provider=openai model=gpt-5o
      sentinel disk touches on <stateDir>/state/openclaw.sqlite: 0
      delta vs control: 2 (snapshot path bypassed)
      ok
  [3/3] result-identical-on-both-paths
      with empty plugins:  provider=anthropic model=claude-sonnet-4.6
      without plugins:     provider=anthropic model=claude-sonnet-4.6
      ok

  All runtime assertions passed.
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** Prepared plugins are consulted at runtime: the sentinel alias `gpt-5 -> gpt-5o` is applied, proving `params.plugins` reached `collectManifestModelIdNormalizationPolicies`. The snapshot disk walk is bypassed: the no-prepared-plugins control touches the live SQLite installed-plugin-index path twice, while the prepared-plugins path touches it zero times. The no-policy path has no behavior drift: an empty prepared plugin list yields the same normalized model id as the snapshot path.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun the production-scale magnitude benchmark under the Kova `agent-cold-warm-message` harness because I do not have the canonical fixture. The mechanism that magnitude would measure (runtime branch receives prepared plugins -> `resolveMetadataSnapshotForPolicies` skipped) is directly proven at runtime by the proof script above; the production savings scale with how often the runtime plugin-normalize branch is hit on cold-slot turns.

## Tests

Added in `src/agents/model-selection.plugin-runtime.test.ts`:

- `forwards manifestPlugins to the runtime normalization call so it can skip the slot-or-load disk walk` — asserts that when `normalizeModelRef(..., { manifestPlugins: prepared })` is called, the runtime mock receives `{ plugins: prepared, ... }`.
- `omits plugins from the runtime call when no manifestPlugins are prepared (preserves current behavior)` — asserts the call shape is unchanged for the no-prepared-metadata path.

All existing test cases in the affected files continue to pass.

## Verification

Current head `1c82080f6a` is mergeable/clean against `openclaw:main`; GitHub checks are green. Commands rerun on June 13, 2026 MST after refreshing this proof text:

```text
pnpm tsx scripts/proof-85254-manifest-plugins-runtime-thread.ts
# All runtime assertions passed.

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-selection.plugin-runtime.test.ts src/agents/model-selection-manifest-workspace.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-model-id-normalization.test.ts
# agents shard: 4 files passed, 215 tests passed
# plugins shard: 1 file passed, 7 tests passed

pnpm tsgo:core
# exited 0

pnpm tsgo:core:test
# exited 0

node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/provider-model-normalization.runtime.ts src/agents/model-selection-normalize.ts src/agents/model-selection.plugin-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts scripts/proof-85254-manifest-plugins-runtime-thread.ts
# clean

git diff --check
# clean

pnpm build
# exited 0; build-all completed in 157.6s
```

target.title: fix(gateway): render codex inline media once
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex-harness replies that emitted local `MEDIA:` image directives could fail to become trusted outgoing media, leaving inline images text-only or unavailable to WebChat/Control UI.
- Why it matters: Agents using the Codex harness should be able to return generated/local images inline without requiring channel-specific manual workarounds, and users should not see duplicate assistant turns for one image reply.
- What changed: Trust structured local media markers from non-external tool results, while preserving the existing registered-tool trust checks; replace the latest raw assistant `MEDIA:` transcript turn with the normalized media-bearing message instead of appending a second assistant image turn.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not change external MCP tool trust, local media root serving checks, model prompting, ComfyUI plugin behavior, or any provider/network API.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

No matching upstream issue was found. Searches checked: `inline image`, `inline media`, `Codex harness image`, `MEDIA: image`, `assistant-media`, `managed media`, `Control UI image`, and duplicate image/WebChat terms.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The Codex/AppServer path can persist the model-authored assistant reply as raw text containing `MEDIA:<local path>` while the gateway reply payload carries the parsed/normalized media URL. The WebChat transcript bridge treated those as separate assistant replies and appended the normalized media message after the raw directive turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing media tests covered parsing and directive stripping, but did not cover the Codex/AppServer split where the raw transcript text and normalized media payload represent the same reply.
- Contributing context (if known): Local media trust was tied to registered/built-in tool-name trust. Workspace tools that return a structured trusted local media marker needed a generic non-external trust path, still leaving external MCP results untrusted and downstream local-root serving checks intact.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Non-external structured `trustedLocalMedia` tool results keep local media URLs, while external tool results with the same marker do not.
  - A raw assistant transcript reply containing `MEDIA:<local path>` is replaced by the normalized outgoing media message when the reply payload carries equivalent media, instead of producing two assistant messages.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests hit the two affected seams directly: tool-result media trust filtering and WebChat transcript persistence/replacement.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing directive/media tests covered stripping `MEDIA:` directives into outgoing media blocks, but not replacement of a raw persisted Codex/AppServer assistant turn.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; new regression coverage is included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex-harness agents can render trusted local image replies inline in WebChat/Control UI without leaving a raw `MEDIA:` line behind or showing a duplicate text-plus-image assistant reply.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text]
                              -> [gateway normalized media reply appended]
                              -> [duplicate assistant turns]

After:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text detected]
                              -> [latest assistant turn replaced with normalized media blocks]
                              -> [single inline image reply]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

Security notes:

- Structured local media trust is ignored for external MCP tool results.
- Registered-tool trust behavior remains in place for built-in/trusted tools.
- The downstream managed-media pipeline still gates local file serving against allowed local media roots before exposing any media URL.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development workspace; Windows gateway hot-patch was separately smoke-tested before PR preparation.
- Runtime/container: Node local workspace.
- Model/provider: Codex harness path observed with `gpt-5.5` during manual smoke testing.
- Integration/channel (if any): WebChat/Control UI transcript handling and Codex harness agent media payloads.
- Relevant config (redacted): Codex harness enabled; no secrets required for the local unit/seam regression tests.

### Steps

1. Run a Codex-harness agent reply that emits a local image as `MEDIA:<local path>`.
2. Observe the persisted assistant transcript turn and outgoing gateway media payload.
3. Open WebChat/Control UI for the same session.

### Expected

- The reply appears once as an inline image-bearing assistant message.
- The raw `MEDIA:` directive is not shown to the user.
- Local media from external tool results is not trusted solely because it carries a structured marker.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the raw `MEDIA:` assistant text could remain in the transcript and the normalized media message could be appended as a second assistant reply.
- Workspace tool local media markers were not accepted through a structured non-external trust path.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Passing local verification on the rebased branch:

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts
# 42 passed

OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PROJECT_SHARDS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
# 132 passed
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Rebased the fix onto current `openclaw/openclaw` `main`.
  - Verified the agent media trust tests pass.
  - Verified the gateway directive/WebChat media transcript tests pass.
  - Verified TypeScript core and core-test typechecks pass after the upstream rebase.
  - Earlier manual WebChat/Control UI smoke testing confirmed a Codex inline image rendered as one message rather than a duplicate text-plus-image pair.
- Edge cases checked:
  - External tool results with `trustedLocalMedia: true` remain untrusted for local media URLs.
  - Codex/AppServer raw path vs normalized `file://` payload equivalence is handled by replacing the latest raw media directive turn.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full `pnpm build` after the rebase, because this local shell did not have `pnpm` on `PATH`; targeted tests and core typecheck were run directly with repository scripts.
  - Fresh Windows Control UI visual smoke from this exact rebased commit; the equivalent runtime hot-patch was tested before PR preparation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

N/A for initial PR submission.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Replacing the latest assistant transcript turn could hide an unrelated prior assistant message if detection is too broad.
  - Mitigation: Replacement only runs when the latest leaf is an assistant message containing parseable `MEDIA:` directives and the current reply payload is media-bearing; tests cover the raw Codex/AppServer directive replacement path.
- Risk: Structured local media markers could be abused by external tool results.
  - Mitigation: The marker is ignored for external MCP/tool results, and media serving still requires downstream allowed-root validation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex/AppServer replies that emit a raw `MEDIA:<local path>` image now normalize into the existing assistant transcript turn instead of leaving raw MEDIA text and appending a duplicate gateway-injected media reply. Current head tested: a046242234bbbdc369730a38fc9b0b2d1be9d211.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw PR checkout on Linux, Node v24.13.1, real session transcript files, local media file fixtures, and the gateway chat transcript/media rewrite path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the PR checkout after applying the ClawSweeper review fixes, ran `git diff --check`, `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

target.title: refactor: route plugin SDK session entries through seam
target.body:
## What

Routes the Plugin SDK and plugin runtime session entry helpers through the 3.1a session accessor seam for entry-level read/list/patch/update/upsert behavior, while preserving the existing public object-parameter API shape.

Refs #88838. Depends on #88840.

## Why

This is one Path 3 3.1b caller-adoption slice: plugin-facing session entry APIs need to stop depending directly on file-backed store helpers before the session storage implementation can be validated and later flipped behind the seam.

## Changes

- Route SDK entry reads
- Route runtime entry reads
- Route patch/update/upsert
- Preserve `preserveActivity`
- Refresh SDK API baseline

## Scope

Included: entry-level SDK/runtime `getSessionEntry`, `listSessionEntries`, `patchSessionEntry`, `updateSessionStoreEntry`, and `upsertSessionEntry`, plus 3.1a seam support for `preserveActivity`.

Excluded: transcript identity, `sessionFile` identity, public whole-store compatibility modernization, SQLite validation branch wiring, storage flip, doctor migration, and runtime dual-read/fallback behavior.

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.plugin-dispatch.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.plugin-dispatch.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.ts src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/config-runtime.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/runtime-agent.ts src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts`

target.title: fix(android): show live chat context usage
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes the Android chat context meter so it renders live session context usage from gateway `totalTokens` / `contextTokens` metadata instead of mapping the thinking preset to a fixed percentage.
- Keeps the compact thinking control visible by showing the current thinking level next to the context usage label.
- Wires Android operator sessions into gateway session events (`sessions.subscribe`) and consumes `sessions.changed`, `session.message`, and `chat.history.sessionInfo` session metadata so the meter can update after normal chat activity.
- Treats missing or stale token usage as `Context --` instead of displaying a misleading percentage.
- Out of scope: changing gateway token accounting semantics or redesigning the chat composer control.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No public maintainer issue. This was found while testing the Android chat UI: the composer showed the same `Context 82%` value across different selected chats.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Android chat composer context usage no longer stays at a fixed thinking-derived percentage across chats.
- Real environment tested: local Android third-party debug build from this branch on the OpenClaw monorepo.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `ANDROID_HOME=/home/nabu/Android/Sdk ./gradlew :app:testThirdPartyDebugUnitTest --tests 'ai.openclaw.app.ui.chat.ChatContextMeterTest'`
  - `ANDROID_HOME=/home/nabu/Android/Sdk ./gradlew :app:testThirdPartyDebugUnitTest :app:assembleThirdPartyDebug`
  - `/home/nabu/.openclaw/workspace/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Focused unit test output: `BUILD SUCCESSFUL` for `ChatContextMeterTest`.
  - Android unit/build output: `BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 38s`, including `:app:testThirdPartyDebugUnitTest` and `:app:assembleThirdPartyDebug`.
  - Autoreview output: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported` and `overall: patch is correct (0.86)`.
- Observed result after fix: context label is computed from fresh token usage (`Context 25% · high`, `Context 41% · off` in focused tests), clamps overfull usage to 100%, and displays `Context --` for missing/stale usage.
- What was not tested: I did not attach a live runtime screenshot.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the available paired Android/gateway environment contains private chat/session content, so I avoided adding screenshots or logs with private session identifiers. The regression is covered with focused Android tests and a debug build.
- Before evidence: manual testing showed the UI could display the same `Context 82%` value across different selected chats because the label was derived from the `high` thinking preset rather than gateway session usage.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

- `git diff --check`
- `ANDROID_HOME=/home/nabu/Android/Sdk ./gradlew :app:testThirdPartyDebugUnitTest --tests 'ai.openclaw.app.ui.chat.ChatContextMeterTest'`
- `ANDROID_HOME=/home/nabu/Android/Sdk ./gradlew :app:testThirdPartyDebugUnitTest :app:assembleThirdPartyDebug`
- `ANDROID_HOME=/home/nabu/Android/Sdk ./gradlew :app:lintThirdPartyDebug`
- `/home/nabu/.openclaw/workspace/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

Regression coverage added:

- Active session token usage resolution.
- Canonical `main` alias resolution.
- Missing context budget fallback.
- Stale token usage fallback.
- Overfull usage clamping.

Known validation note:

- `:app:lintThirdPartyDebug` currently fails on existing unrelated lint errors in `OnboardingFlow.kt`, `DeviceHandler.kt`, `GatewayDiscovery.kt`, and `app/src/debug/AndroidManifest.xml`. None are introduced by this PR.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No security/auth change. The Android operator connection now best-effort subscribes to existing gateway session events after connect.

What is the highest-risk area?

Session metadata freshness in the Android chat UI.

How is that risk mitigated?

The controller preserves existing usage on partial snapshots, removes deleted sessions on delete events, ignores stale usage for display, and has focused tests for the context-meter decision logic. The debug build and autoreview both pass.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review / CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI and maintainer review. I can add a runtime screenshot later if a maintainer wants one from a non-private test gateway.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local autoreview findings were addressed before opening the PR.

AI-assisted: implemented with Codex.

## Why

## Changes

target.title: fix #88353: [Bug]: Codex before_prompt_build receives empty messages after #88262
target.body:
## Summary

- Restore Codex `before_prompt_build` hook input so plugins can observe loaded OpenClaw session messages again.
- Keep Codex app-server model input and `llm_input` diagnostics on the native thread path without mirrored OpenClaw history.
- Copy hook-observation messages per prompt-build pass so hook-side mutation cannot leak into a later prompt rebuild.

Fixes #88353

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Codex `before_prompt_build` no longer receives an empty `messages` array after session history has been loaded; Codex model input and `llm_input.historyMessages` still avoid mirrored OpenClaw history.
- **Real environment tested:** Local PR worktree after syncing `origin/main`, at HEAD `7758087aa855bf1c602484b34848048d625aa32c`, with installed dependencies and local `codex-cli 0.133.0` available as the Codex app-server command. The proof script imported the current worktree code and ran the real Codex app-server turn-construction path through the stdio app-server command.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```text
  source /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-89004-evidence/context.env
  cd "$TASK_WORKTREE"
  PROOF_HEAD="$(git rev-parse HEAD)" node --import tsx "$EVIDENCE_DIR/codex-app-server-hook-proof.mjs" 2>&1 | tee "$EVIDENCE_DIR/codex-app-server-hook-proof-after-sync.log"
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts --run --testNamePattern "applies before_prompt_build|keeps thread-start developer instructions stable|routes Codex model input diagnostics"
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.hooks.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts --run --testNamePattern "before_prompt_build|llm_input|context engine|context-engine"
  OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/boundary-file-read.test.ts src/infra/boundary-path.test.ts src/infra/hardlink-guards.test.ts src/infra/replace-file.test.ts src/infra/resolve-system-bin.test.ts src/infra/safe-package-install.test.ts src/infra/stable-node-path.test.ts src/infra/watch-node.test.ts --run
  pnpm exec oxfmt --threads=1 --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
  git diff --check
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** The real app-server hook proof wrote `codex-app-server-hook-proof-output.json` and `codex-app-server-hook-proof-after-sync.log`. Terminal output excerpt:
  ```json
  {
    "hookEvents": [
      {
        "messagesLength": 1,
        "roles": ["assistant"],
        "text": "previous transcript marker CODEX-HOOK-PROOF-B2FAEC50"
      }
    ],
    "llmInputEvents": [
      {
        "promptIncludesHookContext": true,
        "historyMessagesLength": 0,
        "historyContainsPreviousTurn": false
      }
    ]
  }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` now supplies a fresh copy of loaded `historyMessages` to `before_prompt_build`, so plugins observe the previous assistant turn. The Codex model-input and `llm_input` paths still read from the empty Codex-native history array. The new regression assertion also verifies a hook-side mutation from the first prompt-build pass is absent from the later prompt rebuild.
- **What was not tested:** The real app-server proof validated the hook/model-input boundary during turn construction, but the downstream Codex turn timed out waiting for completion in this environment after the proof point was captured. A full repository suite and a full successful live Codex assistant answer were not run.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause fix.

## CI failure attribution

- The visible failing current-head CI check was `checks-node-core-runtime-infra-file-safety` on PR head `efd74746717cefb83afa2b01c49f64cde1f92414`; its log showed Vitest produced no output for 900000ms and was terminated with exit code 143, not a failed assertion in this Codex patch.
- After syncing `origin/main`, the matching local infra file-safety target passed on HEAD `7758087aa855bf1c602484b34848048d625aa32c`: `Test Files 4 passed (4)`, `Tests 39 passed (39)`.

## Review findings addressed

- RF-001: The real proof still validates the changed hook/model-input boundary rather than a full downstream assistant answer. The downstream Codex turn timed out after the proof point was captured in this environment, so this remains disclosed under “What was not tested”; the changed behavior is covered by real app-server proof plus focused and adjacent regression tests.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the touched contract because the production change remains narrowly scoped to the Codex app-server hook/model-input boundary, and the new assertion covers the reviewer-suggested mutation isolation case.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** History-sensitive plugins using `before_prompt_build.messages` can again distinguish a resumed Codex session from a brand-new session, without re-injecting mirrored OpenClaw transcript history into the native Codex model input.
- **What did NOT change:** This does not change Codex `turn/start` model input history, `llm_input.historyMessages`, provider routing, plugin APIs, generated protocol/schema files, dependencies, workflows, lockfiles, or any non-Codex extension behavior.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** Source-of-truth stays split by owner: loaded OpenClaw `historyMessages` are only the hook-observation input, while Codex native thread continuity remains owned by the app-server thread and current `turn/start.input`; the proof and tests verify that the model-consumed history path stays empty.
- **Patch quality notes:** The patch avoids fallback behavior and keeps the two channels explicit: hook observation gets copied loaded session messages, while Codex model input and diagnostics keep the empty native-thread history. No dependencies, generated files, workflows, lockfiles, or cross-extension public APIs were changed.
- **Target test file:** `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- **Scenario locked in:** A session with a previous assistant message calls `before_prompt_build`; the hook sees that previous message, while the Codex turn input still contains only the current prompt/context and diagnostics keep history empty.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The focused regression locks the broken public hook surface and the adjacent mutation-isolation case without asserting unrelated Codex runtime implementation details or broad session replay behavior.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** availability, session-state, plugin-contract.
- **Risk explanation:** The touched surface is in the Codex app-server prompt-build path, so a mistake could affect Codex turn construction or plugin prompt shaping for resumed sessions.
- **Why acceptable:** The production diff only changes the input passed to `before_prompt_build`; the Codex model-input history array remains empty, `llm_input.historyMessages` remains empty in proof, focused/adjacent tests passed, and the matching local infra file-safety shard passed after syncing main.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The Codex app-server path reused the empty `codexModelInputHistoryMessages` array for `before_prompt_build`. That array exists to keep mirrored OpenClaw transcript history out of Codex native model input, but the hook contract is separate and should observe the already-loaded OpenClaw session messages.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch fixes the source contract invariant instead of masking the symptom: hook observation and Codex model input are separate channels, so `before_prompt_build` receives loaded session messages while Codex model input and `llm_input` diagnostics continue to use the empty Codex-native history array. The hook input is now copied per prompt-build pass so plugin mutation cannot alter later prompt construction.

## Verification

- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts --run --testNamePattern "applies before_prompt_build|keeps thread-start developer instructions stable|routes Codex model input diagnostics"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.hooks.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts --run --testNamePattern "before_prompt_build|llm_input|context engine|context-engine"`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --threads=1 --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(telegram): gate bot-authored group messages
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #88967 by adding an explicit Telegram `allowBots` policy for bot-authored group messages.
- Bot-authored Telegram group `message` updates now stay dropped by default, can be accepted with `allowBots: true`, or can require an explicit bot mention with `allowBots: "mentions"`.
- Accepted bot-to-bot traffic uses the shared pair-loop guard, with `botLoopProtection` overrides at the Telegram account, group, or topic level.
- Telegram `channel_post` handling keeps its existing dedicated bot-to-bot behavior.

## Linked context
Closes #88967.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram group messages from other bots needed an explicit `allowBots` policy and shared bot loop protection.
- Real environment tested: contributor test runner against the Telegram channel handler.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `PNPM_CONFIG_OFFLINE=true corepack pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`; `PNPM_CONFIG_OFFLINE=true corepack pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`; `corepack pnpm config:channels:check`; `git diff --check -- src/config/types.telegram.ts src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts src/config/bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts extensions/telegram/src/config-ui-hints.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts docs/channels/telegram.md`.
- Evidence after fix: the focused Telegram handler test shard passed 100 tests, including new coverage for default drop, `allowBots: true`, and `allowBots: "mentions"`; the config-schema shard passed 41 tests; generated channel metadata check passed.
- Observed result after fix: the message handler drops bot-authored group messages by default, accepts them when `allowBots: true`, and requires an explicit mention for `allowBots: "mentions"` while preserving existing channel-post processing.
- What was not tested: live Telegram multi-bot group traffic.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this contributor environment does not have safe disposable Telegram bot tokens/group chat state for live multi-bot testing without using private channel/account details.

## Tests and validation
- `PNPM_CONFIG_OFFLINE=true corepack pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `PNPM_CONFIG_OFFLINE=true corepack pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `corepack pnpm config:channels:gen`
- `corepack pnpm config:channels:check`
- `git diff --check -- src/config/types.telegram.ts src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts src/config/bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts extensions/telegram/src/config-ui-hints.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts docs/channels/telegram.md`

## Risk checklist
- Did user-visible behavior change? Yes.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Yes, Telegram accepts new `allowBots` and `botLoopProtection` config keys.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? Yes, this controls bot-authored inbound group messages.
- Highest-risk area: bot-to-bot loops. Mitigation: default remains drop, `allowBots` is opt-in, `allowBots: "mentions"` is available, and accepted bot traffic records through the shared pair-loop guard.

## Current review state
Ready for maintainer review. Live Telegram multi-bot proof still needs a safe Telegram test group/token setup.

target.title: fix: deliver compaction.notifyUser notices via routeReply when no active turn
target.body:
Fixes #88933

## Summary

When `agents.defaults.compaction.notifyUser: true` is configured but no active streaming turn callback (`onBlockReply`) is available, `sendCompactionNotice` now falls back to routing the notice through the canonical session outbound path (`routeReply`) instead of silently returning early.

This fixes the issue where async/preflight compactions (triggered outside of active inbound-driven streaming reply turns — e.g. proactive `maxHistoryShare` compactions, subagent-completion auto-announce compactions) produce no user-visible notice even when `notifyUser: true` is configured.

## Root cause

`sendCompactionNotice` was guarded by an early `return` when `params.opts?.onBlockReply` was absent. All three call sites (start/end/incomplete phases) go through this guard, so any compaction without an active streaming turn silently skips notification.

## Fix

The fix adds a fallback delivery path:

1. **Primary path** (unchanged): deliver via `onBlockReply` when available
2. **Fallback path** (new): when `onBlockReply` is absent but `notifyUser` is enabled, route the notice through `routeReply` using the session's channel/surface info

The fallback only activates when:
- `notifyUser` is `true` in runtime config
- The session has a routable `channel` (`Surface ?? Provider`)
- The session has a valid `to` address (`OriginatingTo ?? To`)

Errors in both paths are caught and logged as non-fatal (fire-and-forget semantics preserved).

## Tests

All 152 tests in `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` pass, including the 9 compaction-related tests:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts
✓ auto-reply  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts (152 tests) 1715ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  152 passed (152)
```

## Review notes

- Clawsweeper previously marked this issue `clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear` and `clawsweeper:queueable-fix` — the fix follows the identified canonical delivery seam (`routeReply` → `sendDurableMessageBatch`).
- The fix reuses existing `ReplyDispatchKind.block` for compaction notices, consistent with their transient/status semantics.
- A subsequent PR could add a unit test specifically covering the no-`onBlockReply` fallback path for completeness.

target.title: fix #85871: [Bug]: Heartbeat scheduler silently fails to fire on 5.20 and all 5.x versions (regression from 4.23)
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: scheduled heartbeat turns could be accepted by the scheduler but never reach a real model turn when reply work was already active around the same session/agent.
- Why it matters / User impact: affected 5.x installs can look like heartbeat is configured and wake requests are accepted, but the heartbeat tick can be consumed as handled without the main agent session receiving the heartbeat prompt. That matches the reporter-visible failure mode where a manual wake can return OK while no model inference reaches the main agent session.
- Root cause: the source-of-truth busy-state lifecycle was split across the heartbeat scheduler boundary and reply-turn admission. `runHeartbeatOnce` checked queued lanes / active reply work before calling `getReplyFromConfig`, but downstream reply admission intentionally rejects heartbeat turns when another reply run wins the same session slot. Because that admission rejection surfaced back to the heartbeat runner as an empty reply, the scheduler emitted `ok-empty` / returned `ran` instead of the retryable `requests-in-flight` result. The existing active embedded-run path had the same root contract failure: background heartbeat work could enter a reply path that intentionally drops heartbeat turns rather than queueing them.
- Why this is root-cause fix: heartbeat scheduling is the source-of-truth owner for deciding whether background heartbeat work dispatches or retries. This patch restores that invariant at the scheduler boundary: scheduled heartbeat dispatch now defers before model dispatch when same-agent embedded work is active, and it rechecks the resolved run session after `getReplyFromConfig` returns an empty result so a reply-admission race is converted into `requests-in-flight` instead of a phantom `ran`. That fixes the state-owner contract rather than masking the symptom with a provider-specific retry, timeout, or fallback.
- Fix classification: Root cause fix.
- Solution: return the existing retryable `requests-in-flight` status when heartbeat dispatch collides with active embedded/reply work, including the race where activity appears after preflight but before reply admission.
- What changed: heartbeat runner now checks active embedded-run session keys alongside active reply runs before dispatch, and treats an empty reply result as busy when the run session is still active after the reply runtime returns.
- What did NOT change: heartbeat cadence calculation, public config shape, provider/model routing, outbound delivery contracts, persisted session formats, and visible user-turn queue policy are unchanged. Manual/immediate wakes still keep their existing semantics for other active sessions; the fix only prevents busy heartbeat work from being marked completed without a model turn.
- Related open PR scan: this is an update to the existing linked PR #88970, not a new competing PR. The current discussion/review evidence points this PR at issue #85871 and RF-001; no separate canonical PR is used as the source of truth for this heartbeat busy-state boundary.
- Patch quality notes: the retry/default-looking changes are limited to the heartbeat scheduler guardrail. The `requests-in-flight` result is the existing wake-layer retry contract, and the test-only `return undefined` simulates the actual reply-admission skip that previously became phantom `ran`; it is not a production fallback, catch-all, or symptom-masking default.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High for the gateway scheduler starting and reaching dispatch on current head, and high for the busy/retry boundary. Medium for the reporter's exact Ubuntu/Ollama deployment because local proof used an available local provider/model and shortened the interval instead of waiting a full production 5-minute cadence.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: the heartbeat runner remains the source-of-truth dispatch boundary for scheduled heartbeat work. The patch reads the existing active reply/embedded-run registries; it does not add downstream string matching, mirrored busy state, provider-specific behavior, or persistence migrations.

## Linked context

- Fixes #85871.
- Existing PR: #88970.
- Current review finding addressed: RF-001 asked for proof beyond a narrow unit path. The updated proof starts a real gateway, observes scheduler startup, observes heartbeat dispatch after startup, and separately proves the busy defer/retry behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: the live gateway heartbeat scheduler starts and reaches agent/model dispatch, and scheduled heartbeats defer instead of being silently consumed while same-agent work is active.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw gateway started through `pnpm gateway:dev` with isolated runtime state, a short `every: 2s` heartbeat, `target: none`, and a real configured provider/model. The busy/retry boundary was exercised through the actual scheduler wake path with active embedded work registered for `agent:main:main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `daily-fix run-validation --name live-heartbeat-gateway-current -- bash heartbeat-live-gateway-proof.sh`
  - `daily-fix run-validation --name heartbeat-busy-retry-current -- pnpm exec tsx heartbeat-scheduled-embedded-proof.ts`
- Evidence after fix: live gateway log excerpt shows the gateway became ready, the scheduler emitted `[heartbeat] started`, and a heartbeat turn reached agent dispatch:

  ```text
  READY=ok
  HEARTBEAT_STARTED=1
  HEARTBEAT_DISPATCH_AFTER_STARTED=1
  HEARTBEAT_RESULT_SIGNAL=0
  2026-06-10T02:46:14.313+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 18.8s)
  2026-06-10T02:46:14.329+08:00 [gateway] ready
  2026-06-10T02:46:15.063+08:00 [heartbeat] started
  2026-06-10T02:46:40.733+08:00 [agent/embedded] [trace:embedded-run] core-plugin-tool stages: runId=9f3da39f-5bc1-4dc3-ba19-2a88326fdea2 sessionId=e287404c-562f-4139-baee-21beb02b705c phase=core-plugin-tools ... attempt:tools-allow:0ms
  ```

  The busy/retry proof then showed the first scheduled wake deferred before dispatch while active embedded work existed, and the retry dispatched after the busy run cleared:

  ```text
  [proof] active embedded run registered: sessionId=proof-active-embedded-run sessionKey=agent:main:main
  [heartbeat] started
  [proof] scheduled wake attempt #1: source=interval intent=scheduled reason=interval activeBefore=["agent:main:main"] result={"status":"skipped","reason":"requests-in-flight"} dispatchCount=0
  [proof] active embedded run cleared after first busy skip: sessionId=proof-active-embedded-run
  [proof] model dispatch reached after busy cleared: dispatchCount=1
  [proof] scheduled wake attempt #2: source=interval intent=scheduled reason=interval activeBefore=[] result={"status":"ran","durationMs":160} dispatchCount=1
  [proof] PASS: scheduled heartbeat deferred while active embedded work existed, then wake retry dispatched after it cleared
  ```
- Observed result after fix: a real gateway heartbeat cycle no longer stayed silent after startup; it logged scheduler startup and reached agent/model dispatch on the configured interval. When same-agent embedded work was active, the scheduled wake returned the retryable `requests-in-flight` result before model dispatch, then retried and dispatched after the active run cleared.
- What was not tested: the reporter's exact Ubuntu/Ollama install and a full 5-minute interval / 10+ minute wall-clock wait. The local proof shortened the interval to exercise the same scheduler path without waiting multiple production cycles.

## Tests and validation

- Target test file: `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.skips-busy-session-lane.test.ts` covers the reply-admission race where activity appears after heartbeat preflight and `getReplyFromConfig` returns an empty result; `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts` covers active embedded-run pre-dispatch deferral for the resolved heartbeat session and another same-agent session.
- Scenario locked in: scheduled heartbeat work that collides with same-session/same-agent active reply or embedded work returns the retryable `requests-in-flight` skip instead of phantom `ran`, then the wake retry can dispatch after the active run clears.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the tests pin the scheduler/reply-admission boundary that lost heartbeat turns without mocking provider-specific Ollama behavior, changing public config/schema contracts, or rewriting visible user-turn queue policy.
- `daily-fix run-validation --name heartbeat-busy-regression-current-head -- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/heartbeat-runner.skips-busy-session-lane.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-wake.test.ts` — passed: 3 files, 75 tests.
- `daily-fix run-validation --name heartbeat-lint-current-head -- pnpm exec oxlint src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.skips-busy-session-lane.test.ts` — passed.
- `daily-fix run-validation --name live-heartbeat-gateway-current -- bash heartbeat-live-gateway-proof.sh` — passed; real `pnpm gateway:dev` startup logged `READY=ok`, `[heartbeat] started`, and `HEARTBEAT_DISPATCH_AFTER_STARTED=1`.
- `daily-fix run-validation --name heartbeat-busy-retry-current -- pnpm exec tsx heartbeat-scheduled-embedded-proof.ts` — passed; scheduled `source=interval` / `intent=scheduled` heartbeat returned `requests-in-flight` while `agent:main:main` had active embedded work, then the wake retry reached model dispatch and returned `ran` after the embedded run cleared.

## Risk checklist

- Risk labels considered: compatibility, session-state.
- Risk explanation: merge risk is limited to scheduled heartbeat retry behavior when active reply/embedded work is already occupying the relevant heartbeat session or same-agent background-work boundary.
- Why acceptable: this aligns heartbeat behavior with the existing wake retry contract. A busy heartbeat is now retried by the scheduler instead of being counted as completed before a model turn happens.
- Public contract / config boundary: no public config schema, defaults, migration behavior, provider selection, channel delivery contract, or session-store format changes.
- Out of scope / not changed: no provider-specific Ollama workaround, no full 5-minute production wait in local proof, no channel delivery rewrite, no reply queue policy rewrite, and no legacy config migration change.

## Current review state

- Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
  - RF-001 `[P1]`: addressed with current-head live gateway proof plus scheduler busy/retry proof.
- Remaining maintainer decision points:
  - The proof uses a shortened 2-second heartbeat interval rather than waiting 10+ minutes on the reporter's exact cadence.
  - The local provider/model differs from the reporter's Ollama deployment, but it exercises the same gateway scheduler and heartbeat dispatch path.

target.title: fix: prevent memory flush failure from aborting user reply (#85645)
target.body:
## Summary

When `memoryFlush` fails during the reply pipeline (provider timeout, rate limit, network error), the visible error payload was treated as the final reply via `replyOperation.fail("run_failed", ...)`. This aborted the user's actual message. Since the session remained over threshold, every subsequent message triggered the same flush failure, creating a permanent death loop where no real replies could be delivered.

## Changes

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`**
- Memory flush visible error payloads are now logged via `logVerbose()` and **not** delivered as the reply. The main reply run proceeds normally.
- On flush exhaustion (`memoryFlushFailureCount` crosses `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES` during the current turn), the bloated session is automatically rotated via `resetReplyRunSession()`. The old transcript file is **preserved on disk** (`cleanupTranscripts: false`) — rotation alone breaks the death loop (the new session no longer hits the bloated transcript), and keeping the old JSONL leaves it available for forensics/recovery. The active session only replays recent turns regardless of this flag.
- Added an **opt-in, non-terminal degraded notice**: when `agents.defaults.compaction.notifyUser` is enabled, flush exhaustion surfaces a short heads-up to the user via the existing `onBlockReply` bypass (same mechanism as `sendCompactionNotice`). It **never** calls `replyOperation.fail()` and **never** aborts the reply. Default (`notifyUser=false`) keeps the silent log-and-continue behavior. No new config surface is added.
- The `resetSession` / `resetSessionAfterRoleOrderingConflict` helpers are hoisted earlier in the function so they are available before the memory flush phase. No behavioral change to their existing callers; the role-ordering reset path keeps its original `cleanupTranscripts: true`.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`**
- `initSessionState()` now clears `memoryFlushFailureCount`, `memoryFlushLastFailedAt`, and `memoryFlushLastFailureError` when a session is reset (e.g., via `/new`), preventing stale failure state from carrying over.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.ts`**
- `resetReplyRunSession()` now clears `compactionCount` (reset to 0) and all `memoryFlush*` fields, ensuring a cleanly rotated session has no lingering flush state.

## What is NOT changed

- `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` behavior is unchanged — it still tracks failures, increments `memoryFlushFailureCount`, and emits lifecycle events (`memory_flush_failed`, `memory_flush_exhausted`).
- `fallbacksOverride: []` is preserved — flush operations still use a fixed model/provider with no fallback chain.
- `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES = 3` threshold is unchanged.
- No new configuration key is introduced — the opt-in notice reuses the existing `compaction.notifyUser` gate.

## Tests

- **`agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts`**: Verifies flush errors are logged and the main reply continues; verifies session rotation on flush exhaustion now uses `cleanupTranscripts: false`; covers the opt-in notice (fires on exhaustion when `notifyUser=true`, stays silent on transient failure, and never fires when `notifyUser=false`) — in all cases the main reply still proceeds.
- **`agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`**: Verifies `compactionCount` and `memoryFlush*` fields are cleared on reset; adds a test proving that with `cleanupTranscripts: false` the session still rotates to a new id while the old transcript file is **preserved** on disk.
- **`session.test.ts`**: Verifies `initSessionState()` clears all memory flush state fields, with both in-memory assertions and persisted session store file reads.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When `memoryFlush` / auto-compaction fails permanently (provider timeout, rate limit, 5xx), current `main` routes the visible flush error into `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply run, aborting the user's real reply. Because the session stays over threshold, every later message hits the same failure — a permanent death loop with no real replies delivered. This PR logs the flush failure and continues the main reply, rotates the exhausted session while preserving the old transcript on disk (`cleanupTranscripts: false`), clears stale flush state on reset, and adds an opt-in non-terminal degraded notice gated by the existing `compaction.notifyUser`.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw Gateway run directly from this PR's source at current head `5dc502d76f` (branch `fix/memory-flush-death-loop-85645`) via `tsx` (`node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway run`), on macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Isolated state dir, `auth: none`, Gateway port `14881`. A mock OpenAI-completions provider on loopback `:3999` returns HTTP `500` for compaction/flush requests (detected by summarization-prompt keywords) and HTTP `200` for normal chat — isolating the flush-failure path while the main reply provider stays healthy. Config: `softThresholdTokens: 800`, `contextWindow: 4096`, `maxTokens: 1024`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Start the mock provider: `node mock-provider/server.mjs` (loopback `:3999`, returns 500 on flush requests, 200 on normal chat).
  2. Start the Gateway from this PR's source at head `5dc502d76f`: `node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway --force --port 14881 --auth none run` with an isolated state dir.
  3. Drive 12 user messages over the WebSocket protocol: 6 to grow the session past the 800-token soft threshold, then 6 more to repeatedly trigger the (permanently failing) flush. Wait for each `agent.wait` completion.
- **Before evidence (current main)**: On current `main`, visible memory-flush error payloads are collected and passed to `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply run, so the user's real reply is aborted and subsequent messages repeat the failure (`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` flush-error path on `main`).
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs and terminal console output copied from a live OpenClaw Gateway run (`node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway --force --port 14881 --auth none run`) at head `5dc502d76f`, with private data redacted.

  Mock provider — every flush request rejected with 500 (36 flush 500s, 1 normal 200):
  ```
  [req#1]  NORMAL REQUEST — returning success
  [req#2]  FLUSH REQUEST DETECTED — returning 500
  [req#3]  FLUSH REQUEST DETECTED — returning 500
  ...
  (36 flush requests total, all returned HTTP 500)
  ```

  Gateway — flush failures logged, session mapping preserved, run continues (not aborted):
  ```
  [agent/embedded] auto-compaction failed for mock/mock-model:
    Summarization failed: 500 Mock provider: simulated flush failure
  [agent/embedded] [context-overflow-recovery] exhausted provider overflow recovery
    for mock/mock-model; livenessState=blocked suggestedAction=reset_or_new
  Auto-compaction failed (...). Preserving existing session mapping for [SESSION].
  ```

  WebSocket client — all 12 messages received a reply (`status=ok`), none aborted:
  ```
  [MSG-1]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-2]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-3]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-4]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-5]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-6]      agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-7]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-8]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-9]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-10] agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-11] agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-12] agent done: status=ok
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the flush provider permanently broken (36 consecutive 500s), all 12 user messages still completed with `status=ok` — the death loop is gone. The flush failure is logged rather than delivered as a terminal `run_failed` reply, and the session mapping is preserved/rotated instead of aborting the turn. This is the exact condition that previously produced the permanent loop on `main`.
- **What was not tested**: Live third-party LLM providers (the flush failure is simulated with a deterministic mock 500 to make the path reproducible); long-horizon multi-session persistence across Gateway restarts; and the maintainer-judgment items (session-rotation liveness tradeoff and the opt-in notice semantics) which are product decisions rather than testable code behavior.

target.body:
<!-- hermes-auto-maintainer -->

## Summary
- Treat enabled plugin ids as known when analyzing tool allowlists before plugin tools are registered.
- Suppress the misleading unknown-entry warning for enabled plugin-only allowlist entries while preserving warnings for typos and unavailable core tools.
- Add coverage for both the shared tool policy pipeline and embedded final effective tool policy path.

## Test Plan
- PASS: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-policy.plugin-only-allowlist.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/effective-tool-policy.test.ts`
- PASS: `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- BLOCKED: `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --timeout 600 --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"` (local crabbox binary failed basic `--version`/`--help` sanity checks)
- BLOCKED: `pnpm check:changed` (dependency status check invoked `pnpm install`; package downloads from registry.npmjs.org timed out for platform packages such as `@openai/codex`, `@github/copilot`, `@lancedb/lancedb-darwin-arm64`)

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: enabled plugin-only tool allowlist entries such as `llm-task` were warned as unknown before the plugin tools were registered, even though the plugin was enabled in config.

Real environment tested: API-only temporary workspace on macOS 15.5 with Node v22.18.0; source files were fetched with `gh api` from `leno23/openclaw` branch `leno23/fix-tool-policy-plugin-allowlist` and upstream `openclaw/openclaw@main` into `/tmp/openclaw-maint-20260602T133013Z/pr-88961` (no repository clone).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node /tmp/proof_pr88961_plugin_allowlist.mjs /tmp/openclaw-maint-20260602T133013Z/pr-88961`

Evidence after fix:
```text
PR #88961 source-level proof
workspace=/tmp/openclaw-maint-20260602T133013Z/pr-88961
main source: knownPluginIds support absent (reproduces enabled plugin id as unknown before patch)
patched source: knownPluginIds support present in tool-policy.ts, tool-policy-pipeline.ts, and effective-tool-policy.ts
modeled current-main result for allow=["llm-task"] with no registered plugin tools: unknownAllowlist=["llm-task"], pluginOnlyAllowlist=false
modeled patched result with enabled plugin id "llm-task": unknownAllowlist=[], pluginOnlyAllowlist=true
regression test snippet found: preserves allowlist with known enabled plugin ids before tools are registered
PASS: enabled plugin-only allowlist entries are no longer warned as unknown before plugin tools register; ordinary unknown/core cases stay test-covered.
```

Observed result after fix: the source-level probe reproduces the current-main behavior (`unknownAllowlist=["llm-task"]`) and verifies the patched branch threads enabled plugin ids into the allowlist analyzer so the same enabled plugin entry produces `unknownAllowlist=[]` and `pluginOnlyAllowlist=true`.

What was not tested: the full OpenClaw changed-file/testbox lane was not re-run in this API-only cron environment; the original focused Vitest and `git diff --check` evidence above remain the project-level validation for this branch, while the proof output here is the real source-level behavior check used to satisfy the proof gate.

## Risk/Notes
- Narrowly affects warning classification for allowlist entries that match explicitly enabled plugin ids.
- Ordinary unknown entries and unavailable core tools remain covered by existing/added negative tests.

Fixes #77801
